elements.sent-2411-001: Federated learning (FL) is an emerging paradigm for training machine learning models across distributed clients.
elements.sent-2411-002: Traditionally, in FL settings, a central server assigns training efforts (or strategies) to clients.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, from a market-oriented perspective, clients may independently choose their training efforts based on rational self-interest.
elements.sent-2411-004: To explore this, we propose a potential game framework where each client’s payoff is determined by their individual efforts and the rewards provided by the server.
elements.sent-2411-005: The rewards are influenced by the collective efforts of all clients and can be modulated through a reward factor.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our study begins by establishing the existence of Nash equilibria (NEs), followed by an investigation of uniqueness in homogeneous settings.
elements.sent-2411-007: We demonstrate a significant improvement in clients’ training efforts at a critical reward factor, identifying it as the optimal choice for the server.
elements.sent-2411-008: Furthermore, we prove the convergence of the best-response algorithm to compute NEs for our FL game.
elements.sent-2411-009: Finally, we apply the training efforts derived from specific NEs to a real-world FL scenario, validating the effectiveness of the identified optimal reward factor.
elements.sent-2411-010: Federated learning (FL) \cite{mcmahan2017communicationefficient, zhang2023understanding has shown considerable promise in training large-scale machine learning models across distributed clients without compromising the privacy of their raw data.}
elements.sent-2411-011: In FL, clients train local models using their respective local datasets and transmit only the updated model parameters, rather than the raw data, to the central server for aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-012: To improve learning performance such as model accuracy, it is essential to enlist a sufficient number of clients with diverse data sources \cite{kairouz2021advances .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Existing research on FL algorithm design primarily focuses on optimizing learning performance, assuming that enough clients are willing to participate and contribute to the training process \cite{li2020federateda, mcmahan2017communicationefficient, song2024fedadmm .}
elements.sent-2411-014: However, in practical scenarios, clients may be hesitant to engage in FL without appropriate compensation due to the following reasons.
elements.sent-2411-015: Firstly, training a local model entails the consumption of the client's local computational resources and data (although not in raw form).
elements.sent-2411-016: Secondly, clients also face the potential risk of data leakage, as FL remains vulnerable to privacy threats such as data reconstruction attacks \cite{ren2022grnn, zhu2019deep .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Hence, an incentive mechanism is necessary to incentivize clients to produce the desired local updates and allocate rewards by leveraging clients' contributions \cite{tu2022incentive .}
elements.sent-2411-018: In FL, self-interested clients are more incentivized to contribute when offered higher rewards.
elements.sent-2411-019: Meanwhile, the server may be constrained by a limited monetary budget to incentivize clients.
elements.sent-2411-020: To address this multi-agent interaction and decision-making problem, economic and game-theoretic approaches \cite{barron2013game, osborne1994course have gained significant attention.}
elements.sent-2411-021: The task of FL can be formulated as the following minimization problem: \begin{equation}\label{prob_FL} \min_{\theta \in \mathbb{R}^n} \sum_{i=1}^m \rho_i F_i(\theta), \text{ where } \rho_i = \frac{D_i}{D}, \text{ for } i \in [m].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-022: Here, $m$ denotes the number of clients, $[m] \coloneqq \{1, \ldots, m\}$ is the set of client indices, $\theta \in \mathbb{R ^n$ represents the trainable parameters, $D_i$ is the size of the dataset for client $i$, and $D = \sum_{i=1 ^m D_i$ represents the total size of all datasets combined.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: The function $F_i$ denotes the local loss function for client $i$, typically defined as the empirical risk over its private dataset.
elements.sent-2411-024: For solving \eqref{prob_FL , the training paradigm of FL involves multiple communication rounds, in which clients perform local training while the server conducts global aggregation.}
elements.sent-2411-025: To be concrete, at round $t \in [T]$, each client $i \in [m]$ performs local training in parallel as follows: \begin{equation}\label{local_update} \theta_i^{t+1} = \operatorname{Update}_i(\theta^t, s_i^t),\end{equation} where $\operatorname{Update _i$ is an abstract update rule that depends on global model parameters $\theta^t$ and the client's training {effort (or strategy) $s_i^t$ (e.g., the number of local epochs, gradient descent steps, or mini-batch sizes, see \Cref{rem_local_update for more details).}}}
elements.sent-2411-026: After completing the local training, each client sends its updated local model parameters $\theta_i^{t+1}$ back to the server for aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-027: For instance, in the most commonly used FedAvg algorithm \cite{mcmahan2017communicationefficient , the server aggregates these parameters using $\theta^{t+1 =\sum_{i=1 ^m \rho_i \theta_i^{t+1}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Finally, the server broadcasts $\theta^{t+1}$ to each client for the next round of training.
elements.sent-2411-029: Therefore, we make a heuristic assumption in this article that FL training performance is positively correlated with clients' average training effort $\bar{s}$, defined as: \begin{equation}\label{eq_avg_s} \bar{s} = \sum_{i=1}^m \rho_i \bar{s}_i, \,\, \text{where } \bar{s}_i = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^T s_i^t.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-030: In a typical FL training process, the server assigns the clients' local training efforts, which is analogous to a planned economic system.
elements.sent-2411-031: However, given the inherent limitations and varying capabilities of each client, market-oriented decision-making can be more practical.
elements.sent-2411-032: For example, smaller clients may lack the necessary computational resources to fully implement the training efforts assigned by the server.
elements.sent-2411-033: In this game, each client seeks to maximize its payoff function, which is determined by its local training cost and the reward received from the server.
elements.sent-2411-036: Each client's payoff function $P_i: \mathcal{S \to \mathbb{R}$, with $\mathcal{S = \prod_{i\in [m] \mathcal{S _i$, given by \begin{equation}\label{eq_payoff_P_i} P_i(s_i, s_{-i}) = \sum_{t=1}^T r_i(s_i^t,s_{-i}^t) - c_i(s_i^t).\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: In the payoff function \eqref{eq_payoff_P_i , $c_i(s_i^t)$ is the training or computational cost incurred by client $i$ for employing the effort $s_i^t$, while $r_i(s^t_i, s^t_{-i )$ denotes the reward offered by the server to compensate the client's cost.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: The reward depends on both the client's effort $s^t_i$ and the efforts $s^t_{-i}$ of other clients.
elements.sent-2411-039: Specifically, the cost $c_i(\cdot)$ and the reward $r_i(\cdot,\cdot)$ considered in this article have the following quadratic forms (see Remarks \ref{rem_cost and \ref{rem_reward for further explanations): \begin{equation}\label{eq_cost_i} c_i(s_i^t) = \alpha_i (s_i^t)^2, \label{eq_reward1} r_i(s_i^t,s_{-i}^t) = p^t s_i^t, \,\, where p^t = \lambda \sum_{j=1}^m \rho_j s_j^t.\end{equation}.}}

Therefore, in this article, a key challenge we address is to determine an optimal reward factor $\lambda^*$.
elements.sent-2411-051: Meanwhile, we investigate typical game-theoretic concerns, such as the existence and uniqueness of the NE, as well as numerical algorithms for computing it.
elements.sent-2411-052: The main results of this article, addressing the above questions, are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-053: In this setting, we prove in Theorem \ref{thm_uniq that the NE is unique, except in the case where the parameter $\lambda$ takes on a critical value $\lambda^*$, as defined in \eqref{lambda_2_equation .}}
elements.sent-2411-054: At this critical point $\lambda^*$, the game can have infinite NEs.
elements.sent-2411-055: The critical reward factor $\lambda^*$ acts as a jump point, where $\bar{s}$ experiences a sharp increase, making $\lambda^*$ the optimal choice for the server's reward factor.
elements.sent-2411-056: Additionally, we introduce two other thresholds, $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ (see \eqref{eq_lambda_1_2 ), which represent the activation and saturation points for the training efforts, as illustrated in \Cref{fig_thresholds .}}
elements.sent-2411-057: We then apply clients' rational training efforts (i.e., the NE of the game) under different reward factors to a real-world FL training scenario.
elements.sent-2411-058: The results show that while increasing the reward factor enhances training performance, the most significant improvement occurs at the critical jump point $\lambda^*$, providing strong numerical evidence for the optimality of $\lambda^*$ (see \Cref{fig_FL_train ).}
elements.sent-2411-059: It is important to reiterate that our work offers a mathematical perspective on the interplay between clients' training efforts and the server's incentives, paving the way for further exploration of more complex FL systems and networks with positive consumption externalities.

elements.sent-2411-060: Current game theory-based incentive mechanisms in FL primarily analyze clients' behaviors by formulating and solving a Stackelberg game \cite{von2010market .}
elements.sent-2411-061: In a Stackelberg game, the players designated as leaders make their moves first, followed by the followers, who act after observing the leaders' decisions.
elements.sent-2411-062: Therefore, it is also termed as a leader-follower two-level game.
elements.sent-2411-063: The solutions to the payoff maximization problems of the leaders and the followers construct the Stackelberg equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-064: One of the early explorations of Stackelberg game formulation in FL is in \cite{khan2020federated , where the server acts as the leader, and the clients are the followers.}
elements.sent-2411-065: The server as the leader first sets a monetary reward to the clients.
elements.sent-2411-066: In the lower-level subgame, given the server’s reward, each client determines its local training effort to maximize its payoff function.
elements.sent-2411-067: The server is assumed to know the clients' best-response strategy and payoff functions.
elements.sent-2411-068: Then, in the upper-level subgame, the server optimizes a strategy to maximize its payoff based on the best-response of the clients.
elements.sent-2411-069: In other words, the server (leader) in a Stackelberg game needs to solve a bi-level optimization problem, which yields a Stackelberg equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-070: Similarly, in \cite{lee2020market , a two-level Stackelberg game involving multiple mobile edge computing devices as clients and a coordinator as the server is proposed and a unique Stackelberg equilibrium is given as a closed form.}
elements.sent-2411-071: Furthermore, \cite{dong2020federated extends the FL architecture to a learning market, incorporating service users who are willing to pay for the global model trained by the clients and the server.}
elements.sent-2411-072: A three-level hierarchical Stackelberg game model is proposed to simulate the market-oriented interactions among the above three types of participants.
elements.sent-2411-073: The backward induction method \cite{aumann1995backward is used to derive the analytical Stackelberg equilibrium of the hierarchical Stackelberg game.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Despite the progress mentioned above, the Stackelberg game formulation suffers from several drawbacks.
elements.sent-2411-075: On the one hand, the leader in the upper-level subgame must possess complete information about the followers' payoff functions in the lower-level subgame to derive the Stackelberg equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-076: This necessitates the disclosure of sensitive and private information by the clients, which contradicts the fundamental privacy principles of FL.
elements.sent-2411-077: On the other hand, existing approaches overlook the constraints of clients' training resources, which can significantly impact their strategies throughout the training process.
elements.sent-2411-078: To tackle these issues, several works \cite{he2023game, tahanian2021game, zhang2023robust have considered using general non-cooperative games to model FL.}
elements.sent-2411-079: However, some mathematical challenges related to NEs, particularly the uniqueness and convergence of computational algorithms, remain insufficiently addressed in these studies.
elements.sent-2411-080: Potential games \cite{monderer1996potential (see Definition \ref{def_potential_game ) offer a promising framework for tackling these challenges.}}
elements.sent-2411-081: In such games, NEs can be characterized as stationary points of potential functions, simplifying both analysis and computation.
elements.sent-2411-082: A commonly used numerical method to compute NEs in potential games is the best-response algorithm \cite{durand2016complexity, monderer1996potential, swenson2018best , which is based on the best-response mapping introduced by Nash \cite{nash1950equilibrium .}}
elements.sent-2411-083: The potential games, despite their particular structure, have been widely applied to model problems in various fields, such as multi-agent control \cite{liu2019game,britzelmeier2019numerical and resource allocation \cite{wu2020potential, raschella2020dynamic , highlighting their versatility and effectiveness in modeling real-world scenarios.}}
elements.sent-2411-084: In this work, we explore the possibility of formulating the FL training process within a potential game framework, examining the interplay between clients' local training efforts and the server's incentives.

elements.section-2411-002: A potential game framework for federated learning
elements.sent-2411-100: In this section, we first provide a detailed explanation of the cost-incentive mechanism described in \eqref{eq_cost_i -\eqref{eq_reward1 from the introduction.}}
elements.sent-2411-101: Next, we introduce the corresponding game formulation as described in \Cref{def_FL_game .}

elements.sent-2411-103: As a distributed system designed to solve the optimization problem \eqref{prob_FL , the conventional FL training process does not provide clients with the flexibility to choose their training efforts.}
elements.sent-2411-104: To address this, we introduce a cost-incentive mechanism in \eqref{eq_cost_i -\eqref{eq_reward1 .}}
elements.sent-2411-105: Let us describe the FL training process, incorporating the cost-incentive mechanism, in the following summarized workflow.
elements.sent-2411-106: A graphical illustration is also provided in \Cref{fig_workflow .}
elements.sent-2411-107: At each communication round $t \in [T]$, the training-cost-incentive workflow of FL is outlined as follows: Clients side: In parallel, each client $i \in [m]$ selects a training effort $s_i^t$ and performs local training (see \Cref{rem_local_update ).}
elements.sent-2411-108: Then, the client sends the updated local model parameters $\theta_i^{t+1}$ along with his training effort $s_i^t$ to the server for aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-109: Server side: The server aggregates clients' local model parameters and training efforts {using weights $\rho_i = D_i/D$ defined in \eqref{prob_FL , which represent the proportion of client $i$'s data size $D_i$ relative to the total data size $D$ across all clients.}}
elements.sent-2411-110: The server updates the global model parameters: \begin{equation}\theta^{t+1} = \sum_{i=1}^m \rho_i \theta_i^{t+1};\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-111: The server determines the unit rewarding price using a chosen reward factor $\lambda$: \begin{equation}p^t = \lambda \sum_{i=1}^m \rho_i s_i^t.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-112: Then, the server pays each client $i \in [m]$ a reward of $p^t s_i^t$ and simultaneously broadcasts $\theta^{t+1}$ to each client for the next round of training.

elements.subsection-2411-009: Computation of NEs via the best-response algorithm
elements.sent-2411-180: The best-response algorithm \cite{durand2016complexity, monderer1996potential, swenson2018best is a widely employed and easily implemented numerical method to find an NE for potential games.}
elements.sent-2411-181: Here, we present the best-response algorithm adapted to our framework, as outlined in Algorithm \ref{alg_BRA .}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Best-response algorithm}\label{alg_BRA} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Initialization: $s^{0} \in \mathcal{S}^h$; \For{$k = 1, \dots$} \For{$i = 1, \dots, m$} \If{$s_i^{k-1} \notin \arg\max_{s_i \in \mathcal{S}_i^h} P_{i}^h(s_1^k, \dots, s_{i-1}^k, s_i, s_{i+1}^{k-1}, \dots, s_m^{k-1})$} \State $s_i^k \in \arg\max_{s_i \in \mathcal{S}_i^h} P_{i}^h(s_1^k, \dots, s_{i-1}^k, s_i, s_{i+1}^{k-1}, \dots, s_m^{k-1})$; \Else \State $s_i^k = s_i^{k-1}$; \EndIf \EndFor \If{$s^k = s^{k-1}$} \State return $s^k$. \EndIf \EndFor \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-182: A classical convergence result of Algorithm \ref{alg_BRA pertains to finite potential games \cite[Cor.\@ 2.2, Lem.\@ 2.3]{monderer1996potential .}}
elements.sent-2411-183: We extend this result to continuous potential games under certain assumptions, as detailed in Assumption \ref{ass_continuous .}
elements.sent-2411-184: These results offer convergence guarantees for Algorithm \ref{alg_BRA when applied to our FL games.}

elements.sent-2411-186: We have the following theorem on the convergence of Algorithm \ref{alg_BRA applied to the game $\Gamma_{\textnormal{FL ^h$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-212: In this section, we conduct numerical simulations of our FL games.

elements.sent-2411-218: In this subsection, we elaborate on the experimental settings and also summarize them in \Cref{tab_FL_params .}
elements.sent-2411-219: We consider a homogeneous game scenario with $m=20$ clients and $T=50$ communication rounds.
elements.sent-2411-220: The server selects a reward factor $\lambda \in [0, 5]$, while each client has a local computational cost factor $\alpha_i \in [1, 2]$.
elements.sent-2411-221: Each client's training effort $s_i$ is defined as the number of local training epochs, with the specific ranges $[q_i, Q_i]$ indicated in the corresponding tests.

elements.sent-2411-222: The FL task we consider is the image classification problem with the MNIST dataset \cite{lecun1998gradientbased .}
elements.sent-2411-223: The MNIST dataset consists of grayscale images of handwritten digits from 0 to 9 (10 labels) with a size of $28 \times 28$ pixels.
elements.sent-2411-224: We select $D = 10,000$ training samples, distributing them evenly among 20 clients in a non-independent and identically distributed (non-IID) manner, meaning each client has $D_i = 500$ data points with at most two labels.
elements.sent-2411-225: We also use 10,000 test samples to evaluate the prediction accuracy of the trained model.
elements.sent-2411-226: For the FL algorithm, we use FedAvg \cite{mcmahan2017communicationefficient , assuming that all clients are active in each round, performing $s_i$ epochs of local training with a batch size of 250 and a learning rate of 0.01.}
elements.sent-2411-227: The neural network used by each client is a convolutional neural network (CNN) {with two convolutional layers, comprising 32 and 64 channels, respectively, as the same to that in \cite{mcmahan2017communicationefficient .}}
elements.sent-2411-228: Each client employs the cross-entropy loss function and the ReLU activation function.
elements.subsection-2411-012: The evolution of NEs with respect to different reward factors
elements.sent-2411-229: In this subsection, we validate our theoretical results in \Cref{subsec_uniq_homo by examining the relationship between the clients' average training effort $\bar{s}$ and the server's reward factor $\lambda$.}
elements.sent-2411-230: We consider two scenarios in which each client has a different range of training efforts.
elements.sent-2411-231: In Scenario 1, the minimum training effort for each client is fixed at $q_i = 1$, while in Scenario 2, $q_i$ is uniformly sampled from the range $[1,20]$.
elements.sent-2411-232: The maximum training effort is set to $Q_i \in [20,30]$ in both scenarios, with other parameters remaining the same as indicated in \Cref{subsec_simu_param .}
elements.sent-2411-233: We compute the NE using Algorithm \ref{alg_BRA and present the results in \Cref{fig_NE_zoom_out and \Cref{fig_NE_zoom_in .}}}
elements.sent-2411-234: We emphasize that Scenario 1 is more prevalent in practical FL applications than Scenario 2, since the training effort corresponds to the number of local epochs, with a natural lower bound of one and a typical upper bound greater than one.
elements.sent-2411-235: In our simulation for Scenario 1, the constant $ c_1 = 1.37 $ is less than $ c_2 = 14.81 $, which aligns with the conditions in Theorem \ref{thm_uniq (2b).}

elements.sent-2411-237: This comparison highlights distinct behaviors of NEs under differing parameter settings.
elements.sent-2411-238: As shown in \Cref{fig_NE_zoom_out , in both scenarios, the average effort $\bar{s}$ increases with the reward factor $\lambda$ and exhibits similar behavior in the initial and final stages.}
elements.sent-2411-239: Before the activation point $\lambda_1$, $\bar{s}$ remains at its minimum value $\bar q$ as shown in Figures \ref{fig_NE_zoom_in_a and \ref{fig_NE_zoom_in_d .}}

elements.sent-2411-242: However, the evolution of $\bar{s}$ between $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ differs notably between the two scenarios, particularly around the critical jump point $\lambda^*$.These differences are outlined as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-243: In Scenario 1, where $c_1<c_2$, $\bar{s}$ exhibits a significant jump from $c_1$ to $c_2$ at $\lambda^*$.

elements.sent-2411-250: Overall, these results highlight the sensitivity of clients' training efforts to the server's reward factor across different scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-251: We identify the optimal reward factor for the server as a value slightly larger than the jump point $\lambda^*$, where a sharp increase in the average training effort is observed.

elements.subsection-2411-013: The training performance of FL across various reward factors
elements.sent-2411-253: In this subsection, we evaluate the FL training performance under different reward factors to further validate our proposals for the server's selection of the reward factor $\lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-254: Specifically, we select four representative reward factors around the three critical thresholds identified in Scenario 1 (refer to \Cref{fig_NE_zoom_out_a ), as detailed in \Cref{tab_four_cases .}}
elements.sent-2411-255: The results of FL training in four cases are presented in \Cref{fig_FL_train .}
elements.sent-2411-256: These cases examine how varying the server’s reward factor $\lambda$ influences clients’ training efforts and, more importantly, the resulting FL training performance.
elements.sent-2411-257: In the tests, we apply clients' rational efforts $(s_i)_{i\in[m]}$ across these four cases to FL training, with detailed training settings provided in \Cref{subsec_simu_param .}
elements.sent-2411-258: In all tests, each client performs $\lceil s_i \rceil$ epochs of local training, where $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ denotes the ceiling function (i.e., $\lceil x \rceil$ is the smallest integer no less than $x$).
elements.sent-2411-259: We present in \Cref{fig_FL_train the results of FL training, depicted through accuracy and loss curves, across the four different cases.}
elements.sent-2411-260: The observations are outlined as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-261: In Case 1 (selected before the activation point $\lambda_1$), the model shows the worst performance, with both accuracy and loss curves reflecting suboptimal training due to insufficient clients' training efforts.
elements.sent-2411-262: In Case 2 (selected before the jump point $\lambda^*$), there is a slight improvement in performance compared to Case 1, but the gains are very limited, indicating that the marginal increase in training effort is not enough to significantly enhance model accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-263: Notably, in Case 3 (selected after the jump point $\lambda^*$), there is a significant improvement in training performance, marked by a sharp increase in accuracy and a corresponding decrease in loss.
elements.sent-2411-264: In Case 4 (selected after the saturation point $\lambda_2$), further increases in training effort lead to only marginal improvements in accuracy and a slight reduction in loss.
elements.sent-2411-265: This indicates that beyond the jump point, as clients further increase their training efforts, the improvements in model performance gradually plateau, reflecting diminishing returns.
elements.sent-2411-266: Overall, these results suggest that while increasing the reward factor $\lambda$ can improve training performance, the most significant gains are achieved around the jump point $\lambda^*$.
elements.sent-2411-267: Beyond this point, additional incentives from the server yield diminishing returns.
elements.sent-2411-268: Therefore, an optimal strategy for the server would be to select a reward factor slightly above the jump point $\lambda^*$ to maximize model performance while minimizing unnecessary incentive expenditure.

elements.sent-2411-404: In this paper, we introduce a potential game framework to model the federated learning (FL) training-cost-incentive process.
elements.sent-2411-405: We prove the existence of Nash equilibria (NEs) in the proposed game and further investigate the uniqueness of the NE in a homogeneous scenario where clients maintain constant efforts throughout the training process.
elements.sent-2411-406: Additionally, we characterize the evolution of NEs in relation to the server's reward factor, identifying three critical thresholds: the activation point, the jump point, and the saturation point.
elements.sent-2411-407: Notably, our findings emphasize the significance of the jump point, where a sharp increase in clients' training efforts is observed, indicating it as the optimal reward factor for the server.
elements.sent-2411-408: Meanwhile, extensive numerical experiments strongly support our theoretical results.
elements.sent-2411-409: Overall, our work provides a robust foundation for understanding the interplay between clients' training efforts and the server's incentives.
elements.sent-2411-410: For future work, we propose the following perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-411: A first extension can focus on more general heterogeneous scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-412: For instance, it is reasonable to incorporate a discount factor into the server's reward scheme based on the number of rounds.
elements.sent-2411-413: This aligns with the convergence properties of widely used stochastic gradient algorithms in machine learning, where significant gains in model performance are typically observed during the early training stages \cite[Sec.\@ 4]{bottou2018optimization .}
elements.sent-2411-414: This phenomenon is also reflected in our simulation results shown in \Cref{fig_FL_train , where major improvements are made in the first several rounds.}
elements.sent-2411-415: Furthermore, adding a discount factor may also help address the issue of non-uniqueness of the NE in heterogeneous cases, as the counterexample mentioned in Remark \ref{rem_non_unique no longer applies in this scenario.}
elements.sent-2411-416: A second perspective focuses on the asymptotic behavior of our FL game as the number of clients $m$ approaches infinity.
elements.sent-2411-417: In this scenario, a nonatomic version of $\Gamma_{\text{FL }$ or $\Gamma^h_{\text{FL }$ will be analyzed.}}
elements.sent-2411-418: The NEs in this setting are characterized by an optimality condition over the distribution of training efforts among a continuum of clients, see \cite[Thm.\@ 1]{cheung2018nonatomic .}
elements.sent-2411-419: It is worth noting that proving the uniqueness of NE in the nonatomic case is generally challenging when the price increases with respect to the aggregate, as discussed in \cite[Prop.\@ 3]{cheung2018nonatomic .}
elements.sent-2411-420: Our techniques used in proving the uniqueness results in Section \ref{sec_proof can be naturally adapted to these nonatomic games.}
elements.sent-2411-421: Another promising direction is to explore more complex and practical reward allocation schemes in the FL game formulation.
elements.sent-2411-422: In our current work, after determining the unit price, each client's revenue is linearly correlated with its effort.
elements.sent-2411-423: However, the true relationship between a client’s training effort and the resulting improvement in model performance is unknown and difficult to express analytically.
elements.sent-2411-424: Some attempts have been made to evaluate each client’s contribution (see \cite{liu2022gtg, xue2021toward ).}
elements.sent-2411-425: However, incorporating these evaluations into the game formulation and analysis remains a challenging problem.
